Crt.vot LecuNe NIcusr
CITY COUNCIL
via Federal Express
ApriI 15, 19 92
scott Hallabrin, Esq
Acting ceneral i"""='"r
Fair PoIitical Practices Comrnission
428 ttJtt Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 
9 5814
Re: Request for FPPC Written Response to euestions
Regarding Potential Conflicts of Interest by Irlenbers
the City of Laguna Niguel City Council participating
Decisions Concerning SettLement of Lawsuit in Which
Both They and the City are Defendants
27821LaPazRod . 
Laguna N8uel, Califomiz92656
v4.G1.7141 . F}J<1n4.643.9qV 
:
Patricia C. Bates
iZ 
Paul M. Christiansen
James E Krembas, Ed.D.
larry A. Porter
Thomas !7 !0ilson
ofin
Dear llr. HaLlabrin:
. 
The undersigned is the City Attorney of the City of Laguna
Niguel. The purpose of this letter is to request a written
response from the Fair Political Practices Commission (rFppcr) on
questions concerning whether four of the five members of the eity
of Laguna NigueJ. city Council rrould have a conflict of interest
under the Political Reform Act of 1974 in participating in a
decision regarding the settlenent of a pending lawsuit-against
the city of Laguna Niguel in which they are also narned
defendants.
Below we set out the facts concerning this matter. Also, we
set out three specific questions regarding this factual situation
and the undersigned,s initial 
response to each of the questions.
The undersigned, during the week of ltarch 30, !992,
discussed this natter with John Wallace of the Legal Division.
l.lr. Wallace gave the undersigned his thoughts regirding this
natter and provided copies of a couple of FppC advice ietters
concerning governing body mernbers pirticipating 
in decisions
concerning litigation 
in which they are involvea. However, the
factuar situations presented in those retters are different frorn
that presented in this matter. Ur. wallace suggested that we
consider requesting written advice on this natter, and he
indicated that it lrould take approxirnately Zr woriing days to
prepare such a written response after the Fppcrs receipt-of the
written request.


scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair PoIitical Practices commission
April 15, 1992
Page 2
FACTS
The City of Laguna Niguel was forned on Decenber 1, 1989.
The fornation of the City had been approved by the voters at the
November 1989 general election. AIso, at that election, the
voters elected a five member City Council. Elected as Council
nembers were Patricia Bates, PauI Christiansen, James Krembas,
Larry Porter and Tom Wilson. One of the unsuccessfut candidates
was Eddi,e Rose .
Essentially, since the formation of the City, Mr. Rose has
continually criticized actions of the city in general, and, in
particular, actions of Council nembers Bates, Krenbas, Porter and
Wil-son. The criticism has been in the form of letters to the
editor of ne\rrspapers and in rrhand outsrr that he distributed
around the city, including leaving copies on car windshields
during city events. In october 1990, !tr. Rose initiated a recall
against the four Council members. That recall terminated in
March 1991, when he failed to obtain a sufficient number of
recall petition signatures against any of the four Council
nembers to qualify the recall for subrnission to the voters.
In JuIy 1,991, Ur. Rose filed a clain against the City of
Laguna Nigue1 for $10 nillion a11e9in9 that the four Council
menbers had defaned hin relative to certai,n activities of theirs
done in opposition to Rose,s recall against then. His clain was
rejected by operation of 1aw. on January 23, L992, ttr. Rose
filed a CornpJ.aint entitled Eddie Rose v. Citv of Laguna Niquel:
Thomas W. Wilson; Linda S. Bavme i James R. Krenbas: Jeanette M.
Krembas; Patricia C. Bates: Larrv A. Porter. Nena B. porte
Dennis A. Harris; A1fred Chiurzaai; Lacruna Niquel for Ton Wilson
#891579 et a1.; orange County Superior Court Case No. 67 95 37.
Mr. Rose a]leges in his Cornplaint that he was defamed by the
varj,ous anti-recalL activities alleged in the Complaint. Mr.
Rose clained compensatory damaqes and punitive damages in the
amount of $1 niI]ion. 
Further, he alleged that 'rmost named
defendants were and are employees of defendant City of Laguna
Niguel and performed the above-described acts during the course
and scoPe of their enplo),ment for the City of Laguna Niguel.tt
The defendants in the Lawsuit are the City, the four Council
members, the carnpaign committee of one of the four Council
nembers, the wives of Council rnenbers Krenbas and porter who were
apparently serving as their campaign treasurers, Linda Bayne vho
was serving as the carnpaigTn treasurer for Council nember igil"o,
and who hras on the city,s public Safety Commj-ssion and Special


Scott Eallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
April 15, 1992
Page 3
Events Conmittee, and Dennj,s Harris and Al Chiurzaai who rrere
apparently supporters of the antj--recall campaign and who also
respectively were on the Cityrs Pub1ic Safety Cornmission and the
Senior Citizens Conmittee.
The defense of the City was tendered to the Southern
California Joint Povers Insurance Agency ('TSCJPIAI) which is a
joint powers agency consisting of a number of cities to provide
joint legal defense and indemnification from tort claims. 
SCJPIA
accepted the defense and retained the law firrn of Wood & Morimoto
to defend the city. 
The named individual defendants are each, at
this tine, providing for their own defense.
wood & l.lorimoto, on behalf of the City, directed
correspondence to llr. Rosers attorney basically stating that
there was no City involvernent in the actions alleged in the
Conplaint, and suggesting that if Mr. Rose imrnediately disnissed
the City frorn his laqrsuit, the City might be willing to waive
litigation 
costs incurred in the defense of the City. At the
same t j.rne, Wood E l{orimoto propounded substantiat discovery to
the plaintiff,s 
attorney seeking the factual basis of the
allegations in the Complaint to the effect that the named
defendants were acting within the course and scope of their
ernployrnent with the City in taking the actions alteged in the
Complaint. Mr. Rose,s attorney responded to the above-nentioned
Wood & Morimoto letter and discovery requests by proposing to
disniss the city in return for the City waiving litigation 
costs
and malicious prosecution claims.
The settlement of !1r. Rose,s lawsuit against the City with a
waiver by the City of defense costs and malicious proseculion
cl-airns requires approval by the City Council. The proposed
settlenent has raised the question of whether the city council
should approve the proposed settlernent, reject it, or approve a
counter-proposal such as the City agreeing to a waiver of
litigation 
costs and malicj.ous prosecution clairns in return for
Mr. Rose paying part of the costs incurred by the City.
OUESTIONS
1.
v. citv of Lasuna Niquel.
The primary
members, who are
question presented
named as individual is whether the
defendants in four Council
the lawsuit,


scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices conmission
April 15, 1992
Page 4
may participate in the decision regarding the proposed settlement
of the lairsuit by Ur. Rose against the City?
In approaching this issue, ue attempted to ascertain whether
the four Council nenbers have an econonic interest on vhich a
decision regarding the settlernent of the BgqC lalrsuit against the
city i.rould have a reasonably foreseeable material financiaL
effect. Of the various potential econonic interests
(investt0ents, interest in real property, sources of incohe,
business positions and gifts) the one that appears to pertain to
this situation is lrhether a decision night increase or decrease
the personal expenses, assets, or liabilities of the individual
Council members by $250 or more. The question that we attempted
to ansver was, nore specifically, whether or no! at this tine
(i.e., the tine the decision on the proposed settlenent woutd be
nade) the settlenent of the case against the City (or the
rejection of settlement or approvaL of a counter-proposal ) woutd
reasonably foreseeably increase or decrease the expenses of the
four Council nembers in defending theuselves in the B9SC lavsuit
by $25O or nore. We atternpted to ascertain such things as
vhether, if the case vere settled and one defendant, namely the
City and its attorneys, rr/ere removed fron the lawsuit, might that
be a financiat benefit to the renaining individual defendants.
It might make the lavsuit less costly to defend in terms of less
attorney tirne spent conmunicating with other attorneys, and less
costs of service of documents. Also, we attempted to ascerlain
whether such a settlenent might nake it more coslIy for the
individuals to defend the lavsuit. Having the City and its
attorneys in the lawsuit niqht possibly result in some sort of an
overall cost savings by sone sort of nutual sharing of defense
expenses. Conversely, if the lawsuit were not settled on behalf
of the city, it night be rnore expensive for the j"ndivj.dual
defendants because of having to continue to deal lrith the City
and its attorneys, or it night be less expensive if some sort of
cost sharing arrangement might be accornplished.


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Cornnission
ApriL 15, 199 2
Page 5
lve came to the conclusion that it rras not reasonably
foreseeable that a decision, lrhether it be settlement or
rejection of settl,enent, would have an irnpact of $250 or more on
the individual defendants. First, it was sirnply too speculative
as to whether either the settlenent or rejection of settlement
would increase or decrease the overalL costs of defense. To
analyze such a question, one would have to make guesses at
various intangible aspects of handling litigation, 
such as
v/hether, as a general proposition, nore attorneys in a lawsuit
makes defense more expensive, or whether in this case any sort of
cost sharing is possible. Second, with the costs of defense for
the individual defendants spread out over nine individuals with,
we understand, seven of them being represented by one law firm,
and two of them being represented by another law f j,rm, it would
be irnpossible to ascertain whether total cost savings (or a loss)
would be as nuch as $250 or more per person. Therefore, the
undersigned,s concl-usion is that it was not reasonably
foreseeable at this tine that participatj-ng in naking a decision
on the proposed settlement of the subject case bet$/een Eddie Rose
and the City of Laguna Niguel would cause an increase or decrease
of $250 or more on the personal expenses, assets and liabilities
of individual Council nenbers, and thus, they were not prohibited
from participating in the rnaking of such a decision.
AIso, we understand that if, in fact, there would be a
conflict of interest on the part of the four city council rnernbers
in participating in the decision regarding the proposed
settlement of the Rose case, then two of the Council nembers,
chosen by lot, could, under exj.sting FPPC regulations, be used to
constitute a quorun of three of the five Council menbers, and, as
part of that quorurn, ful1y participate in the discussion and
decision regarding the proposed settlernent. We would appreciate
a confirrnation of this understanding of the FppC regulations.
2. 
Participatino in Decisions Reqardinq Appointrnent of a
Settlement Committee.
A couple of the City Council mernbers have raised the
possibility of the council appointing a citizens, cornmittee to
review and make recommendations to the City Council regarding the
settl-ement of the lawsuit.
.Wou1d participating in naking a decision to appoint such a
committee and then participating in the appointnent of the
committee members violate the conflict of interest provisions of


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices coDmission
April 15. 1992
Page 5
the Political Reforn Act?
It 6eens to the undersigned that the anaLysis rrould be the
same as that regarding the first question in that if such a
conmittee i.rere established and its DeEbers appointed, and it made
recommendations reqarding the settlenent of the subject lavsuit,
the Council would have to act on those recornmendations. I do not
believe the Council has the authority to delegate to such a
conmittee, the settlenent of the tawsuit. Thus, ultinately the
council rtould have to nake the decision on the proposed
settLenent. Therefore, the counciL vouLd still be faced with the
analysis of determining irhether or not accepting the committee,6
reconmendation, lrhatever the recorunendation might be, would have
a reasonabLy foreseeable affect on their personal assets of more
than $250 or nore.
3. 
Participatinq in Decisions Reqardinq the Defense and
Indennificati.on of Individuat council Members.
Sone of the Council nehbers have asked whether the City is
obl.igated to provide them with a defense and indennification as
naned defendants in the Rose lawsuit? The plaintiff in his
Complaint does allege that nost of the individual defendants,
withou! identifying which ones, were acting withi.n the course and
scope of their enploynent when taking the actions alleqed in the
complaint, nanely naking the various allegedly defanatory
statenents. However, it also appears fron the facts atteged that
the individuals were not acting within the course and scope of
their enplol.hent lrith the City, but, in fact, were acting
relative to their anti-recall canpaigns. At this point, all of
the naned individual defendants have undertaken their own
defense. However, it is very possible that one or more of the
individuals ni1I tender their defense to the City. Ultimately,
the City Councj.l may be caLLed upon to make a decision as to
vhether or not to provide at City expense the defense and
indennification of the four individual. City Council menbers and
possibly other of the named defendants who vere serving on
various city conmj.ssions and committees.
Would it be a violation of the conflict of interest
provisions of the political Reforrn Act for the Council nembers to
participate.i,n naking a decision regarding the city paying for
their individual defense and indennification in thi iole iawsuitZ
We understand that the FppC has rendered various advice


scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Fair Political Practices conmission
April 15, 1992
Page 7
letterE to the effect that in situations where a governing body
Denber is naDed as a defendant in a lavsuit, he is not prohibited
by the Political Reforn Act fron participating in decisions
regarding lrhether the public agency providing, at its expense, a
defense and indemnification from the claims alleged in the
lai,suit. Further, we understand that this advi.ce is based on the
PPPC.s application of Requlation No. 18700(d) (3). That
regulation specificalLy excludes from the definition of
"decisions'r, in vhich pubtic ehployees are prohibited frorn
participating, decisions regarding the terms and conditions of
their enpto)dneht. Decisions regarding whether a public entity
would provide defense and indeMification are considered by the
FPPC to be decisions regarding the terms and conditions of the
elected off icial's emplo).ment.
Relative to this question, we request a confirnation that
the FPPC would consider that the participation by the four
council nenbers in a decision regarding their indennification and
defense i{ould not be in violation of the conflict of interest
provisions of the Politica1 Reforn Act.
CONCLUSION
we request your lrritten response to each of the three
questions set out above. Tine is of the essence regarding this
matter. Presently, the settlenent proposal described in this
letter is stil1 pending. The case j-s not set for triaL, but rrith
the various I'fast trackrr procedures that have been instituted by
the courts for expediting litigation, this matter does need to be
resolved fairly soon (1) to avoid costs of litigation that vould
be saved if the settLenent is effectuated, or (2) to undertake
necessary defense activities if no settlenent is achieved. lie
vould appreciate it if you could provide us vith sone idea as to
the tining of a response to this letter.
If you require any additj.onal infornation or clarification
regardi.ng the request contained in this Letter, please do not
hesitate to contact the undersigned.

The undersigned is the City Attorney of the City of Laguna Niguel. The purpose of this letter is to request a written response from the Fair Political Practices Commission (rFppcr) on questions concerning whether four of the five members of the eity of Laguna NigueJ. city Council rrould have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act of 1974 in participating in a decision regarding the settlenent of a pending lawsuit-against the city of Laguna Niguel in which they are also narned defendants. Below we set out the facts concerning this matter. Also, we set out three specific questions regarding this factual situation and the undersigned,s initial response to each of the questions. The undersigned, during the week of ltarch 30, !992, discussed this natter with John Wallace of the Legal Division. l.lr. Wallace gave the undersigned his thoughts regirding this natter and provided copies of a couple of FppC advice ietters concerning governing body mernbers pirticipating in decisions concerning litigation in which they are involvea. However, the factuar situations presented in those retters are different frorn that presented in this matter. Ur. wallace suggested that we consider requesting written advice on this natter, and he indicated that it lrould take approxirnately Zr woriing days to prepare such a written response after the Fppcrs receipt-of the written request. scott Hallabrin, Esq. Fair PoIitical Practices commission April 15, 1992 Page 2 FACTS The City of Laguna Niguel was forned on Decenber 1, 1989. The fornation of the City had been approved by the voters at the November 1989 general election. AIso, at that election, the voters elected a five member City Council. Elected as Council nembers were Patricia Bates, PauI Christiansen, James Krembas, Larry Porter and Tom Wilson. One of the unsuccessfut candidates was Eddi,e Rose . Essentially, since the formation of the City, Mr. Rose has continually criticized actions of the city in general, and, in particular, actions of Council nembers Bates, Krenbas, Porter and Wil-son. The criticism has been in the form of letters to the editor of ne\rrspapers and in rrhand outsrr that he distributed around the city, including leaving copies on car windshields during city events. In october 1990, !tr. Rose initiated a recall against the four Council members.
